pattern_recognition: Backtracking - This problem is a classic example of generating combinations with constraints. The signals are the need to balance opening and closing parentheses while ensuring that at no point do we have more closing than opening parentheses.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Generate all possible combinations of parentheses and filter out the invalid ones.
approaches.algorithm: Use recursion to build all combinations of parentheses and check each combination for validity.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use backtracking to build valid combinations directly, avoiding the generation of invalid combinations.
approaches.algorithm: Recursively add '(' and ')' to the current combination while ensuring the count of each remains valid.

dry_run: For n = 2, start with an empty string. Add '(', then recursively add '(', resulting in '((('. Backtrack and add ')' to get '(()'. Continue this process until all combinations are formed: '()()', '(())'.
edge_cases: n = 0 should return an empty list. n = 1 should return ['()']. Large n values may lead to performance issues due to exponential growth in combinations.
common_pitfalls: 1) Forgetting to check the balance of parentheses during generation. 2) Not handling the base case correctly in recursion. 3) Overlooking the need to limit the number of opening and closing parentheses.
